elements.sent-2411-001: The iopart-num \BibTeX{ style is intended for use in preparing manuscripts for Institute of Physics Publishing journals, including Journal of Physics.}
elements.sent-2411-002: It provides numeric citation with Harvard-like formatting, based upon the specification in ``How to prepare and submit an article for publication in an IOP journal using \LaTeXe'' by Graham Douglas (2005).
elements.sent-2411-003: The iopart-num package is available on the Comprehensive \TeX{ Archive Network (CTAN) as /biblio/bibtex/contrib/iopart-num.}
elements.sent-2411-004: To use the iopart-num style, include the command \verb+\bibliographystyle{iopart-num + in the document preamble.}
elements.sent-2411-005: The reference section is then inserted into the document with the command \verb++, where the names of the necessary \BibTeX{ database files should be listed between the braces.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Further general instructions on using \BibTeX{ may be found in the \BibTeX{ documentation.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: The iopart-num style is compatible with, but does not require, the iopart document class.
elements.sent-2411-008: For documents prepared with the natbib package, natbib should be loaded with the options \verb+square+ and \verb+sort compress+ to insure proper formatting of the references in the text.
elements.sent-2411-009: If, instead, documents are prepared using the iopart class but without natbib, the section header for the references must be manually inserted, with the command \verb+
elements.sent-2411-010: Under IOP style conventions, journal names should be set in italic type.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, for journals with multiple lettered sections, the IOP convention is that the journal section letter should appear in roman type after the main journal name, e.g., ``J.\ Phys.\/ A''.
elements.sent-2411-012: Most existing \BibTeX{ styles do not make special provision for lettered sections.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Therefore, typically, the section letter is either included as part of the journal name.
elements.sent-2411-014: The iopart-num style instead introduces a new optional field \verb+section+ which can be used to specify a journal section letter.
elements.sent-2411-015: This section letter is set in roman type.
elements.sent-2411-016: Moreover, if the section letter already appears in any of the usual locations in the database entry (at the end of the journal name, before the volume number, or after the volume number), iopart-num will recognize it and suppress its printing.
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, when you are creating the \BibTeX{ database entry for an article in a lettered journal section, you can still include the section letter in the \verb+journal+ or \verb+volume+ fields, for use with other \BibTeX{ styles, without adversely affecting the formatting for IOP journals.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, the entry for Ref.~\cite{caprio2005:coherent can be generated with.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Note that section names longer than a single letter are also supported (e.g., ``Phys.. Rev.\/ ST Accel.
elements.sent-2411-020: Journal issue numbers are not customarily included in references to journal articles under the IOP formatting conventions.
elements.sent-2411-021: Therefore, the iopart-num style ignores the \verb+number+ field for articles.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, in some periodicals (such as popular magazines or certain journal online supplements), pagination restarts from 1 with each issue.
elements.sent-2411-023: For such periodicals, the issue number is an essential part of the bibliographic information needed to identify an article.
elements.sent-2411-024: The iopart-num style therefore supports an additional field \verb+issue+ in the \BibTeX{ database entry, which can be used to enforce printing of the issue number.}
elements.sent-2411-025: If a value is specified for \verb+issue+, this value is included included parenthetically after the volume number in the reference, as in Ref.~\cite{zamfir2005:132te-beta-enam04 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: The IOP guidelines distinguish between a volume in a {\it series and a volume of a {\it multivolume book or {\it set .}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: For a volume in a series, the series title and volume number are given in parentheses after the book title~\cite{ex8 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: For an individual volume in a multivolume book, the book title is given first, followed by the volume number and volume title~\cite{ex9 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The iopart-num style supports an additional field \verb+volumetitle+ in the \BibTeX{ database entry, which can be used to specify the title for an individual volume of a multivolume book, as in refs.~\cite{ex9,bohr1998:v2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: For example, the entry for Ref.~\cite{bohr1998:v2 is generated with.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In contrast, most existing \BibTeX{ styles allow you to reference a volume of a multivolume book by specifying \verb+title+ and \verb+volume+, as in refs.~\cite{ex7,siegbahn1965:v1 , but do not provide for inclusion of any specific title for the individual volume.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: A volume in a series~\cite{ex8,iachello2006:liealg is indicated in iopart-num, as in most other \BibTeX{ styles, by specifying \verb+title+, \verb+volume+, and \verb+series+ in the \BibTeX{ database entry.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The entries in the reference list below provide examples of the formatting of various types of references, of varying complexity, including journal articles, articles in proceedings or collections, books (individual, multivolume, or in a series), theses, and unpublished references.
elements.sent-2411-034: The \BibTeX{ database entries used to generate these examples can be found in the file iopart-num.bib.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Refs.~\cite{ex1,ex2,ex3,ex4,ex5,ex6,ex7,ex8,ex9 are based upon example entries from the IOP guidelines.. }
elements.sent-2411-036: This document describes the preparation of an article using \LaTeXe\ and \verb"iopart.cls" (the IOP Publishing \LaTeXe\ preprint class file).
elements.sent-2411-037: This class file is designed to help authors produce preprints in a form suitable for submission to any of the journals listed in table~\ref{jlab1 on the next page.}
elements.sent-2411-038: You are not obliged to use this class file---we accept submissions using all common \LaTeX\ class and style files.
elements.sent-2411-039: The \verb"iopart.cls" class file is supplied merely as a convenience for those authors who find it useful.
elements.sent-2411-040: This document gives both general advice that applies whatever class file you use, and specific advice that applies if you choose to use \verb"iopart.cls".
elements.sent-2411-041: We also accept submissions in Word format.
elements.sent-2411-042: See elsewhere on this site for guidelines on Word submissions.
elements.sent-2411-043: If you have any queries about this document or any aspect of preparing your article for submission please contact us at the e-mail address given above.
elements.sent-2411-044: Please ensure that you include all of the sources of funding and the funding contract reference numbers that you are contractually obliged to acknowledge.
elements.sent-2411-045: We often receive requests to add such information very late in the production process, or even after the article is published, and we cannot always do this.
elements.sent-2411-046: Please collect all of the necessary information from your co-authors and sponsors as early as possible.
elements.sent-2411-047: The \verb"iopart" \LaTeXe\ article class file is provided to help authors prepare articles for submission to IOP Publishing journals.
elements.sent-2411-048: This document gives advice on preparing your submission, and specific instructions on how to use \verb"iopart.cls" to follow this advice.
elements.sent-2411-049: You do not have to use \verb"iopart.cls"; articles prepared using any other common class and style files can also be submitted.
elements.sent-2411-050: It is not necessary to mimic the appearance of a published article.
elements.sent-2411-051: The advice on \LaTeX\ file preparation in this document applies to the journals listed in table~\ref{jlab1 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: If your journal is not listed please go to the journal website via \verb"http://iopscience.iop.org/journals" for specific submission instructions.

elements.sent-2411-053: Journals which require references in a particular format will need special care if you are using BibTeX, and you might need to use a \verb".bst" file that gives slightly non-standard output in order to supply any extra information required.
elements.sent-2411-054: It is not necessary to give references in the exact style of references used in published articles, as long as all of the required information is present.
elements.sent-2411-055: Also note that there is an incompatibility between \verb"amsmath.sty" and \verb"iopart.cls" which cannot be completely worked around.
elements.sent-2411-056: If your article relies on commands in \verb"amsmath.sty" that are not available in \verb"iopart.cls", you may wish to consider using a different class file.
elements.sent-2411-057: Whatever journal you are submitting to, please look at recent published articles (preferably articles in your subject area) to familiarize yourself with the features of the journal.
elements.sent-2411-058: We do not demand that your \LaTeX\ file closely resembles a published article---a generic `preprint' appearance of the sort commonly seen on \verb"arXiv.org" is fine---but your submission should be presented in a way that makes it easy for the referees to form an opinion of whether it is suitable for the journal.
elements.sent-2411-059: The generic advice in this document---on what to include in an abstract, how best to present complicated mathematical expressions, and so on---applies whatever class file you are using.
elements.subsection-2411-005: What you will need to supply
elements.sent-2411-060: Submissions to our journals are handled via the ScholarOne web-based submission system.
elements.sent-2411-061: When you submit a new article to us you need only submit a PDF of your article.
elements.sent-2411-062: When you submit a revised version, we ask you to submit the source files as well.
elements.sent-2411-063: Upon acceptance for publication we will use the source files to produce a proof of your article in the journal style.
elements.sent-2411-064: When you send us the source files for a revised version of your submission, you should send us the \LaTeX\ source code of your paper with all figures read in by the source code (see section \ref{figinc ).}
elements.sent-2411-065: Articles can be prepared using almost any version of \TeX\ or \LaTeX{ , not just \LaTeX\ with the class file \verb"iopart.cls".}
elements.sent-2411-066: You may split your \LaTeX\ file into several parts, but please show which is the `master' \LaTeX\ file that reads in all of the other ones by naming it appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-067: The `master' \LaTeX\ file must read in all other \LaTeX\ and figure files from the current directory.
elements.sent-2411-068: Most \LaTeXe\ packages can be used if they are available in common distributions of \LaTeXe; however, if it is essential to use a non-standard package then any extra files needed to process the article must also be supplied.
elements.sent-2411-069: Try to avoid using any packages that manipulate or change the standard \LaTeX\ fonts: published articles use fonts in the Times family, but we prefer that you use \LaTeX\ default Computer Modern fonts in your submission.
elements.sent-2411-070: The use of \LaTeX\ 2.09, and of plain \TeX\ and variants such as AMSTeX is acceptable, but a complete PDF of your submission should be supplied in these cases.
elements.sent-2411-071: Figures should ideally be included in an article as encapsulated PostScript files (see section \ref{figinc ) or created using standard \LaTeX\ drawing commands.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Please name all figure files using the guidelines in section \ref{fname .}
elements.sent-2411-073: We accept submissions that use pdf\TeX\ to include PDF or bitmap figures, but please ensure that you send us a PDF that uses PDF version 1.4 or lower (to avoid problems in the ScholarOne system).
elements.sent-2411-074: You can do this by putting \verb"\pdfminorversion=4" at the very start of your TeX file.
elements.sent-2411-075: All figures should be included within the body of the text at an appropriate point or grouped together with their captions at the end of the article.
elements.sent-2411-076: A standard graphics inclusion package such as \verb"graphicx" should be used for figure inclusion, and the package should be declared in the usual way, for example with \verb"\usepackage{graphicx ", after the \verb"\documentclass" command.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Authors should avoid using special effects generated by including verbatim PostScript code in the submitted \LaTeX\ file.
elements.sent-2411-078: Wherever possible, please try to use standard \LaTeX\ tools and packages..
elements.sent-2411-079: You can produce your bibliography in the standard \LaTeX\ way using the \verb"\bibitem" command.
elements.sent-2411-080: Alternatively you can use BibTeX: our preferred \verb".bst" styles are:.
elements.sent-2411-081: For the numerical (Vancouver) reference style we recommend that authors use \verb"unsrt.bst"; this does not quite follow the style of published articles in our journals but this is not a problem.
elements.sent-2411-082: Alternatively \verb"iopart-num.bst" created by Mark A Caprio produces a reference style that closely matches that in published articles.
elements.sent-2411-084: For alphabetical (Harvard) style references we recommend that authors use the \verb"harvard.sty" in conjunction with the \verb"jphysicsB.bst" BibTeX style file.
elements.sent-2411-085: These, and accompanying documentation, can be downloaded from \penalty-10000 \verb"http://www.ctan.org/tex-archive/macros/latex/contrib/harvard/".
elements.sent-2411-086: Note that the \verb"jphysicsB.bst" bibliography style does not include article titles in references to journal articles.
elements.sent-2411-087: To include the titles of journal articles you can use the style \verb"dcu.bst" which is included in the \verb"harvard.sty" package.
elements.sent-2411-088: The output differs a little from the final journal reference style, but all of the necessary information is present and the reference list will be formatted into journal house style as part of the production process if your article is accepted for publication.
elements.sent-2411-089: Please make sure that you include your \verb".bib" bibliographic database file(s) and any \verb".bst" style file(s) you have used.
elements.sent-2411-090: Copyrighted material and ethical policy If you wish to make use of previously published material for which you do not own the copyright then you must seek permission from the copyright holder, usually both the author and the publisher.
elements.sent-2411-091: It is your responsibility to obtain copyright permissions and this should be done prior to submitting your article.
elements.sent-2411-092: If you have obtained permission, please provide full details of the permission granted---for example, copies of the text of any e-mails or a copy of any letters you may have received.
elements.sent-2411-093: Figure captions must include an acknowledgment of the original source of the material even when permission to reuse has been obtained.
elements.sent-2411-094: Please read our ethical policy before writing your article.
elements.sent-2411-095: Please name all your files, both figures and text, as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-096: Use only characters from the set a to z, A to Z, 0 to 9 and underscore (\_).
elements.sent-2411-097: Do not use spaces or punctuation characters in file names.
elements.sent-2411-098: Do not use any accented characters such as \'a, \^e, \~n, \"o.
elements.sent-2411-099: Include an extension to indicate the file type (e.g., \verb".tex", \verb".eps", \verb".txt", etc).
elements.sent-2411-100: Use consistent upper and lower case in filenames and in your \LaTeX\ file.
elements.sent-2411-101: If your \LaTeX\ file contains the line \verb"\includegraphics{fig1.eps " the figure file must be called \verb"fig1.eps" and not \verb"Fig1.eps" or \verb"fig1.EPS".}
elements.sent-2411-102: If you are on a Unix system, please ensure that there are no pairs of figures whose names differ only in capitalization, such as \verb"fig_2a.eps" and \verb"fig_2A.eps", as Windows systems will be unable to keep the two files in the same directory.
elements.sent-2411-103: When you submit your article files, they are manipulated and copied many times across multiple databases and file systems.
elements.sent-2411-104: Including non-standard characters in your filenames will cause problems when processing your article.
elements.sent-2411-106: In addition to the above points, please give each figure file a name which indicates the number of the figure it contains; for example, \verb"figure1.eps", \verb"figure2a.eps", etc. If the figure file contains a figure with multiple parts, for example figure 2(a) to 2(e), give it a name such as \verb"figure2a_2e.eps", and so forth.
elements.sent-2411-107: Please send your submission via the ScholarOne submission system.
elements.sent-2411-108: Go to the journal home page, and use the `Submit an article' link on the right-hand side.
elements.subsection-2411-009: Sample coding for the start of an article
elements.sent-2411-109: The code for the start of a title page of a typical paper in the \verb"iopart.cls" style might read: .
elements.verbatim-2411-007: \documentclass[12pt]{iopart} \title[The anomalous magnetic moment of the neutrino]{The anomalous magnetic moment of the neutrino and its relation to the solar neutrino problem} \author{P J Smith$^1$, T M Collins$^2$, R J Jones$^3$\footnote{Present address: Department of Physics, University of Bristol, Tyndalls Park Road, Bristol BS8 1TS, UK.} and Janet Williams$^3$} \address{$^1$ Mathematics Faculty, Open University, Milton Keynes MK7~6AA, UK} \address{$^2$ Department of Mathematics, Imperial College, Prince Consort Road, London SW7~2BZ, UK} \address{$^3$ Department of Computer Science, University College London, Gower Street, London WC1E~6BT, UK} \ead{williams@ucl.ac.uk} \begin{abstract} ... \end{abstract} \keywords{magnetic moment, solar neutrinos, astrophysics} \submitto{\jpg}
elements.sent-2411-110: The first non-commented line should be \verb"\documentclass[12pt]{iopart " to load the preprint class file.}
elements.sent-2411-111: The normal text will be in the Computer Modern 12pt font.
elements.sent-2411-112: It is possible to specify 10pt font size by passing the option \verb"[10pt]" to the class file.
elements.sent-2411-113: Although it is possible to choose a font other than Computer Modern by loading external packages, this is not recommended.
elements.sent-2411-115: Authors of very long articles may find it convenient to separate their article into a series of \LaTeX\ files each containing one section, and each of which is called in turn by the primary file.
elements.sent-2411-116: The files for each section should be read in from the current directory; please name the primary file clearly so that we know to run \LaTeX\ on this file.
elements.sent-2411-117: Authors may use any common \LaTeX\ \verb".sty" files.
elements.sent-2411-118: Authors may also define their own macros and definitions either in the main article \LaTeX\ file or in a separate \verb".tex" or \verb".sty" file that is read in by the main file, provided they do not overwrite existing definitions.
elements.sent-2411-119: It is helpful to the production staff if complicated author-defined macros are explained in a \LaTeX\ comment.
elements.sent-2411-120: The article class \verb"iopart.cls" can be used with other package files such as those loading the AMS extension fonts \verb"msam" and \verb"msbm", which provide the blackboard bold alphabet and various extra maths symbols as well as symbols useful in figure captions.
elements.sent-2411-121: An extra style file \verb"iopams.sty" is provided to load these packages and provide extra definitions for bold Greek letters.
elements.sent-2411-122: Double-column layout The \verb"iopart.cls" class file produces single-column output by default, but a two-column layout can be obtained by using \verb"\documentclass[10pt]" at the start of the file and \verb"\ioptwocol" after the \verb"" command.
elements.sent-2411-123: Two-column output will begin on a new page (unlike in published double-column articles, where the two-column material starts on the same page as the abstract).
elements.sent-2411-124: In general we prefer to receive submissions in single-column format even for journals published in double-column style; however, the \verb"\ioptwocol" option may be useful to test figure sizes and equation breaks for these journals.
elements.sent-2411-125: When setting material in two columns you can use the asterisked versions of \LaTeX\ commands such as \verb".
elements.sent-2411-126: If you have any problems or any queries about producing two-column output, please contact us at \verb"submissions@iop.org".
elements.sent-2411-127: If you use \verb"iopart.cls", the code for setting the title page information is slightly different from the normal default in \LaTeX.
elements.sent-2411-128: If you are using a different class file, you do not need to mimic the appearance of an \verb"iopart.cls" title page, but please ensure that all of the necessary information is present.
elements.sent-2411-129: The title is set using the command \verb"", where \verb"#1" is the title of the article.
elements.sent-2411-130: The first letter of the title should be capitalized with the rest in lower case.
elements.sent-2411-131: The title appears in bold case, but mathematical expressions within the title may be left in light-face type.
elements.sent-2411-132: If the title is too long to use as a running head at the top of each page (apart from the first) a short form can be provided as an optional argument (in square brackets) before the full title, i.e.\ \verb"".
elements.sent-2411-133: For article types other than papers, \verb"iopart.cls" has a generic heading \verb"\article[Short title]{TYPE {Full title " and some specific definitions given in table~\ref{arttype .}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: In each case (apart from Letters to the Editor and Fast Track Communications) an optional argument can be used immediately after the control sequence name to specify the short title; where no short title is given, the full title will be used as the running head.
elements.sent-2411-135: Not every article type has its own macro---use \verb"\article" for any not listed.
elements.sent-2411-136: A full list of the types of articles published by a journal is given in the submission information available via the journal home page.
elements.sent-2411-137: The generic heading could be used for articles such as those presented at a conference or workshop, e.g. .
elements.sent-2411-138: Acknowledgment of funding should be included in the acknowledgments section rather than in a footnote.
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{\label{arttype}Types of article defined in the {\tt iopart.cls} class file.} \rm \begin{tabular*}{\textwidth}{@{}l*{15}{@{\extracolsep{0pt plus12pt}}l}} \br Command Article type \mr \verb"\title{#1}" Paper (no surtitle on first page) \verb"\ftc{#1}" Fast Track Communication \verb"\review{#1}" Review \verb"\topical{#1}" Topical Review \verb"\comment{#1}" Comment \verb"\note{#1}" Note \verb"\paper{#1}" Paper (no surtitle on first page) \verb"\prelim{#1}" Preliminary Communication \verb"\rapid{#1}" Rapid Communication \verb"\letter{#1}" Letter to the Editor \verb"\article{#1}{#2}" Other articles\\\ (use this for any other type of article; surtitle is whatever is entered as {\tt \#1}) \br \end{tabular*}
elements.sent-2411-139: For the authors' names type \verb"", where \verb"#1" is the list of all authors' names.
elements.sent-2411-140: Western-style names should be written as initials then family name, with a comma after all but the last two names, which are separated by `and'.
elements.sent-2411-141: Initials should {\it not be followed by full stops.}
elements.sent-2411-142: First (given) names may be used if desired.
elements.sent-2411-143: Names in Chinese, Japanese and Korean styles should be written as you want them to appear in the published article.
elements.sent-2411-144: Authors in all IOP Publishing journals have the option to include their names in Chinese, Japanese or Korean characters in addition to the English name: see appendix B for details.
elements.sent-2411-145: If the authors are at different addresses a superscripted number, e.g. $^1$, \verb"$^1$", should be used after each name to reference the author to his/her address.
elements.sent-2411-146: If an author has additional information to appear as a footnote, such as a permanent address, a normal \LaTeX\ footnote command should be given after the family name and address marker with this extra information.
elements.sent-2411-147: The authors' affiliations follow the list of authors.
elements.sent-2411-148: Each address is set by using \verb"" with the address as the single parameter in braces.
elements.sent-2411-149: If there is more than one address then the appropriate superscripted number, followed by a space, should come at the start of the address.
elements.sent-2411-150: E-mail addresses are added by inserting the command \verb"\ead{#1 " after the postal address(es) where \verb"#1" is the e-mail address.}
elements.sent-2411-152: For more than one e-mail address, please use the command \verb"\eads{\mailto{#1 , \mailto{#2 " with \verb"\mailto" surrounding each e-mail address.}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Please ensure that, at the very least, you state the e-mail address of the corresponding author.
elements.sent-2411-154: The abstract follows the addresses and should give readers concise information about the content of the article and indicate the main results obtained and conclusions drawn.
elements.sent-2411-155: It should be self-contained---there should be no references to figures, tables, equations, bibliographic references etc. It should be enclosed between \verb".
elements.sent-2411-156: The abstract should normally be restricted to a single paragraph of around 200 words.
elements.sent-2411-157: We no longer ask authors to supply Physics and Astronomy Classification System (PACS) classification numbers.
elements.sent-2411-158: For submissions to {\it Nonlinearity}\/ we ask that you should supply Mathematics Subject Classification (MSC) codes.
elements.sent-2411-159: MSC numbers are included after the abstract using \verb"\ams{#1 ".}
elements.sent-2411-160: The command \verb"\submitto{#1 " can be inserted, where \verb"#1" is the journal name written in full or the appropriate control sequence as given in table~\ref{jlab1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-161: This command is not essential to the running of the file and can be omitted.
elements.sent-2411-162: Keywords are required for all submissions.
elements.sent-2411-163: Authors should supply a minimum of three (maximum seven) keywords appropriate to their article as a new paragraph starting \verb":" after the end of the abstract.
elements.sent-2411-164: To keep the header material on a separate page from the body of the text insert \verb"" (or \verb"") before the start of the text.
elements.sent-2411-165: If \verb"" is not included the text of the article will start immediately after the abstract.
elements.sent-2411-166: The text of articles may be divided into sections, subsections and, where necessary, subsubsections.
elements.sent-2411-167: To start a new section, end the previous paragraph and then include \verb"\section" followed by the section heading within braces.

elements.sent-2411-250: Sequential numbering (1), (2), etc, is the default numbering system although in \verb"iopart.cls", if the command \verb"\eqnobysec" is included in the preamble, equation numbering by section is obtained, e.g.\ (2.1), (2.2), etc. Equation numbering by section is used in appendices automatically when the \verb" " command is used, even if sequential numbering has been used in the rest of the article.

elements.section-2411-019: Including author names using Chinese, Japanese and Korean characters in submissions to IOP Publishing journals
elements.sent-2411-398: Authors in all IOP Publishing journals have the option to include names in Chinese, Japanese or Korean (CJK) characters in addition to the English name.
elements.sent-2411-399: The names will be displayed in the print issue and the online PDF, abstract and table of contents, in parentheses after the English name.
elements.sent-2411-400: It is the decision of the individual authors whether or not to include a CJK version of their names; for a single article it is not necessary for all authors to include a CJK name if only one author wishes to do so.
elements.sent-2411-401: It is the responsibility of the authors to check the accuracy and formatting of the names in the final proofs that they receive prior to publication.
elements.sent-2411-402: To include names in CJK characters, authors should use the \verb"cjk.sty" package, available from \verb"http://www.ctan.org/tex-archive/language/chinese/CJK/".
elements.sent-2411-403: Users should be aware that this is a very large and complicated package which relies on a large number of fonts.
elements.sent-2411-404: We recommend using a TeX package that includes this package and all of the fonts by default, so that manual configuration is not required (e.g. the TeXLive distribution, which is available on all platforms (Macintosh, Windows and Linux)).
elements.sent-2411-405: The documentation for the \verb"cjk.sty" package gives information on how CJK characters can be included in TeX files.
elements.sent-2411-406: Most authors will find it convenient to include the characters in one of the standard encodings such as UTF-8, GB or JIS, if they have access to a text editor that supports such encodings.

elements.sent-2411-408: To avoid potential problems in handling the CJK characters in submissions, authors should always include a PDF of the full version of their papers (including all figure files, tables, references etc) with the CJK characters in it.

elements.sent-2411-410: This study undertakes a detailed intercomparison of four open-source electricity system capacity expansion models—Temoa, Switch, GenX, and USENSYS—to examine their suitability for guiding U.S.
elements.sent-2411-411: We isolate the effects of model-specific differences on policy outcomes and investment decisions by harmonizing empirical inputs via PowerGenome and systematically defining “scenarios” (policy conditions) and “configurations” (model setup choices).
elements.sent-2411-412: Our framework allows each model to be tested on identical assumptions for policy, technology costs, and operational constraints, thus distinguishing results that arise from data inputs or configuration versus inherent model structure.
elements.sent-2411-413: Key findings highlight that, when harmonized, models produce very similar capacity portfolios under each current policies and net-zero configuration, with less than 1\% difference in system costs for most configurations.
elements.sent-2411-414: This agreement across models allows us to examine the impact of configuration choices.
elements.sent-2411-415: For example, configurations that assume unit commitment constraints or economic retirement of generators reveal the difference in investment decisions and system costs that arise from these modeling choices, underscoring the need for clear scenario and configuration definitions in policy guidance.
elements.sent-2411-416: Through this study, we identify critical structural assumptions that influence model outcomes and demonstrate the advantages of a standardized approach when using capacity expansion models.
elements.sent-2411-417: This work offers a valuable benchmark and identifies a few key modeling choices for policymakers, which ultimately will enhance transparency and reliability in modeling efforts to inform the clean energy transition for clean energy planning.
elements.sent-2411-418: Safely mitigating climate change requires deep decarbonization of energy systems in the next 15–30 years \cite{intergovernmental_panel_on_climate_change_ipcc_summary_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-419: The electricity sector will be key to this transition.
elements.sent-2411-420: To tap into this opportunity, stakeholders need tools to help plan for deep decarbonization of power systems on a national scale \cite{deep_decarbonization_pathways_project_pathways_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-421: Thirteen states, Puerto Rico, and Washington D.C.
elements.sent-2411-422: We are also in a time of rapid cost reductions for low-carbon power technologies due to continued R\&D advances and economies of scale, coupled with substantial uncertainty about grid technology and policy options.
elements.sent-2411-423: Better models can help planners and policy makers prioritize investments that support rapid and reliable decarbonization while minimizing costs and avoiding premature obsolescence and other risks.
elements.sent-2411-424: Power system planning has historically relied on proprietary models that were designed for an electricity system based largely on fossil fuels and are not well-suited for planning clean power systems.
elements.sent-2411-425: For example, wind turbines, solar panels, and batteries are declining in cost every year and are now able to provide power at a lower levelized cost than fossil-fueled generators, yet traditional capacity planning models based on load duration curves (probability distributions of load) cannot accurately model the simultaneous variation in time and space of variable resources (e.g., wind and sun) and load, or the inter-hour coupling introduced by energy storage or demand flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-426: A new generation of well-established open-source electricity system capacity expansion models (CEMs) has been created to aid in both designing power systems served by high levels of renewable energy and in modeling a diverse range of energy pathways.
elements.sent-2411-427: They are often used to simulate consequential policy decisions.
elements.sent-2411-428: However, these models take different approaches, potentially resulting in different findings and creating confusion or uncertainty among users and policymakers.
elements.sent-2411-429: Unexplained differences in results from models that use similar approaches and methods can confound decision making.
elements.sent-2411-430: An improved understanding of why model results differ and whether these differences matter can advance the field and facilitate consensus when making policy decisions.
elements.sent-2411-431: Both empirical and structural assumptions can lead to different model results.
elements.sent-2411-432: Commodity prices and infrastructure costs are examples of empirical assumptions that may vary by model but can be relatively easy to harmonize.
elements.sent-2411-433: The method determining asset retirement, such aging out assets at the end of their service life versus retiring assets based upon economic uncompetitiveness, is an example of structural assumptions that may vary by model.
elements.sent-2411-434: Methods to treat within-model empirical uncertainty and variability, such as sensitivity and Monte Carlo analysis, are both mature and common.
elements.sent-2411-435: These techniques examine how model results vary with differing empirical input assumptions, and, in doing so, can identify which input parameters are most influential or what results are robust to varying model inputs.
elements.sent-2411-436: In contrast, studies rarely perform experiments under a range of different structural configurations, partly owing to the challenge of identifying varying structural methods from the perspective of a singular model.
elements.sent-2411-437: Inter-model comparisons can broaden the scope of structural and empirical modeling choices to encompass those made across different modeling teams.
elements.sent-2411-438: Previous inter-model comparison efforts, such as those by the Stanford Energy Modeling Forum \cite{huntington1982modeling have been instrumental in developing and applying methods to fairly align policy scenarios assumptions across models.}
elements.sent-2411-439: However, these efforts have not yet fully harmonized respective empirical assumptions or structural differences, which limits the ability to isolate the precise drivers of divergent model outcomes \cite{henry_promoting_2021, van_ouwerkerk_impacts_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-440: Henry et al. \cite{henry_promoting_2021 highlighted the often untested influence of structural differences—such as treatment of technology characteristics and optimization approaches—but only for a single simulation period, relatively simplified scenarios, and for only five greenfield generation technologies.}
elements.sent-2411-441: Additionally, van Ouwerkerk et al. \cite{van_ouwerkerk_impacts_2022 demonstrated that structural features, such as the treatment of storage technologies or generator dispatch behavior, can lead to substantial discrepancies in model outcomes even under harmonized scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-442: Similarly, Sánchez-Pérez et al. \cite{sanchez-perez_effect_2022 depicts how the treatment of time drastically affects the optimal investment and operation of long-duration storage, and Jacobson et al. \cite{jacobsonQuantifyingImpactEnergy2024 demonstrates that siting decisions are suboptimal when using lower temporal resolution.}}
elements.sent-2411-443: Hoffmann et al. \cite{hoffmann_review_2024 emphasize that developing shared mathematical structures and consistent modeling frameworks can facilitate clearer communication and understanding among different models, promoting transparency and comparability.}
elements.sent-2411-444: These studies suggest that not only does it remain difficult to fairly compare model results, but that there is also not yet consensus on how to design and execute intercomparison studies.
elements.sent-2411-445: This study performs a rigorous and extensive comparison of four open-source, decarbonization-focused electricity capacity expansion models: Tools for Energy Model Optimization and Analysis (TEMOA)\cite{hunter_modeling_2013 , Switch\cite{johnston_switch_2019 , GenX\cite{jenkins_genx_2024 , and United States Energy SYStem (USENSYS)\cite{lugovoy_usensys_2024 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-446: This effort is unique in several ways.
elements.sent-2411-447: First, we parameterize models with a common set of empirical input assumptions via the PowerGenome data tool \cite{schivley_powergenomepowergenome_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-448: We carefully differentiate between model configurations and scenarios, and define several specific configurations—sets of choices of model features, resolution, etc.—and share them among the models.
elements.sent-2411-449: We define several distinct scenarios of likely interest to policy makers—future worlds with different policy measures, technology availability, etc.—and compare results across models and configurations within each of these scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-450: These elements allow us to distinguish between differences due to models’ internal formulation versus configuration choices or scenario definitions.
elements.sent-2411-451: They also allow us to identify which model features (as chosen in each configuration) are most important for studying different types of scenarios (e.g., current policies versus net-zero decarbonization).
elements.sent-2411-452: Our quantitative results enable us to draw highly specific and defensible conclusions about the source of differences between different modeling efforts and thus make confident policy recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-453: In addition to presenting quantitative model results we also use the intercomparison process to suggest guidelines and more generalizable methods for rendering modeling intercomparisons.
elements.sent-2411-454: We conclude with a summary of key implications and recommendations for consumers of results/analysis from energy systems planning models and users of open-source CEMs.
elements.sent-2411-455: We harmonized and compared four energy systems models representing the electric power system for the continental U.S.: GenX, Switch, USENSYS, and TEMOA.
elements.sent-2411-456: While the models may be coded differently, they all employ optimization to select the least-cost set of energy sources and technologies for energy conversions, transmission, storage, and carbon management that meet electricity demand on an hourly basis, subject to exogenously specified costs, policies, and scenario assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-457: All of them seek to minimize expenditures during the study period, assuming capital costs are amortized over the life of the asset (not paid immediately); when run in multi-period foresight mode, they seek to minimize the discounted total (net present value) of these costs across all years of the study.
elements.sent-2411-458: The study relies on the concepts of "scenarios" (the world we are optimizing for) and "configurations" (choices of time and space resolution and model features to use when setting up the model).
elements.sent-2411-459: The scenarios are used to represent policy questions of interest.In general, users get an answer to a policy question by comparing scenarios to each other, e.g., comparing emissions and costs between the deep decarbonization scenario and the current policies scenario.
elements.sent-2411-460: Solving for different scenarios provides some initial policy insight and also ensures the models are compared when studying the types of scenarios that users might apply them to.
elements.sent-2411-461: Configurations allow us to distinguish between the effect of choices users make when setting up their models from differences in how features are implemented in the models themselves.
elements.sent-2411-462: In other comparison studies, much of what is perceived as “model differences” are actually due to different configuration choices made by different modeling teams, and the models might produce much more similar results if they were set up more similarly.
elements.sent-2411-463: This study eliminates this ambiguity by using the same configurations for all models.
elements.sent-2411-464: This allows us to identify which specific features are important for modeling each scenario effectively, and also to assess the extent to which internal differences between the models drive differences in results.
elements.sent-2411-465: That is, any residual differences when running multiple models with the same configuration for the same scenario must be due to differences in the models’ algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-466: Models were harmonized in three stages: (1) prospectively aligning assumptions and modeling approach prior to optimization; (2) revising models by comparing results for a common base-case scenario and base-case configuration; and (3) revising models by comparing results across a broader set of scenarios and configurations.
elements.sent-2411-467: Prospectively aligning model assumptions: We used the output specifications of PowerGenome \cite{schivley_powergenomepowergenome_2024 as a guide to align model assumptions prior to any simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-468: PowerGenome is a data management system originally developed to provide inputs for GenX.
elements.sent-2411-469: Using the same source data significantly narrows any differences between models.
elements.sent-2411-470: In addition to the assumptions listed in \Tref{table2 , we clustered thermoelectric fossil-based generators within each region based on variability in heat rates and fixed operating costs.}
elements.sent-2411-471: Clustering reduces model dimensionality while preserving influential underlying variability.
elements.sent-2411-472: We then averaged model inputs by region, planning horizon, and either cluster (for thermoelectric fossil generators) or technology (for all other generators).

elements.sent-2411-479: The base scenario examines what is required to follow the most direct feasible path to achieving near-zero carbon in the electricity sector in 2050 and the base configuration includes a subset of features common to all models.
elements.sent-2411-480: Although the decarbonization pathway in this base scenario is aggressive, following the REPEAT study \cite{jenkins_climate_2023 , it includes a buyout price of \$200 per ton for emissions above the targets to account for alternative mitigation strategies.}

elements.sent-2411-482: The primary changes made during this stage included configuring all models to:.

elements.figure-2411-014: \caption{\label{table4}Summary of modeled configurations} \begin{tabular}{@{}p{2in}p{3.75in}} \br Configuration Alternative specifications Base No requirement for unit commitment or limits on power plant ramp rates, minimum load, etc. Plants retire when they reach standard ages. We optimize each study period independently, in succession, without consideration of later periods (myopic). We consider 52 independent weeks, spanning the full year of available weather data Unit Commitment and Ramping Base configuration using linearized unit commitment; including restrictions on ramp rate, min load, up and down time, and startup costs. Economic Retirement Base configuration without age-based retirement of any existing plants; instead we retire generators permanently if the operating costs are higher than the revenue; avoidable costs include fixed O$\backslash$\&M but not capital recovery; capital recovery is amortized over normal life, but then continues through the whole study at the same rate. Short Sample Period Base configuration using 20 weeks sampled from full weather period (with probability weights) instead of 52 weeks to reduce the model size. Multi-Period Foresight Short Sample Period configuration where we co-optimize for all model years together (short sample is needed to reduce allow for multi-period). Operational Simulation Specialized case used to evaluate the performance of proposed plans in a common context. Base configuration, but freeze generation \& transmission decisions based on a previously run capacity expansion model, use limits from the Unit Commitment and Ramping configuration, and consider all 52 weeks of weather. Then run the model to simulate performance of the plan through the year. Any generation shortfalls are priced at \$5,000/MWh. \br \end{tabular}

elements.subsubsection-2411-007: Steady emissions reductions would require a carbon buyout price of at least \$1,000/tonne
elements.sent-2411-513: The net-zero scenarios in this study have an exogenously specified emissions cap with a steep drop from 2027 to 2030.
elements.sent-2411-514: This trajectory is based on results from the net-zero scenario in Princeton’s REPEAT study, which found that the most cost-effective route to net-zero CO$_2$ emissions by 2050 required the electricity sector to reduce emissions earlier and faster than the rest of the economy \cite{jenkins_climate_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-515: In the base case, we allowed buyout from this cap at a cost of \$200 per tonne of CO$_2$.
elements.sent-2411-516: This is close to the current EPA estimate of the social cost of carbon emissions \cite{us_environmental_protection_agency_report_2023 and also the consensus view of the future cost of direct air capture of CO$_2$ \cite{howard_consensus_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-517: Variation in the assumed carbon buyout price has the most significant impact on the net-zero emissions scenario.
elements.sent-2411-518: A buyout price of \$200 per ton leads to a continued, slow decrease in emissions after 2030, although none of the models find that emissions drop below 100 million tonnes by 2050.
elements.sent-2411-519: At this buyout price nearly all capacity growth is in wind, solar, batteries, and CCS.
elements.sent-2411-520: A buyout price of \$50 per ton builds slightly less wind and solar capacity.
elements.sent-2411-521: The models generally favor expansion of conventional natural gas generation in place of CCS, explaining the steady increase in emissions after 2030.
elements.sent-2411-522: In contrast, a price of \$1,000 per ton favors expansions of CCS, wind, solar, and hydrogen, yielding steady emission reductions despite strong load growth.

elements.subsubsection-2411-009: CCS as a technology option in a deep decarbonization scenario when no other cost-effective firm generation source is available
elements.sent-2411-532: All models show similar results: if CCS is not allowed, the ~300 GW of CCS in the base scenario would be replaced by 150 GW more battery, 300 GW more wind, 250 GW more solar, 100 GW more NGCC, and 35 - 58\% more transmission capacity.
elements.sent-2411-533: This results in about 134 million tons more emissions in 2050, 122\% higher than the base case, where CCS is available.
elements.sent-2411-534: Notice that these results hold in the context of: modeling CCS as technology with characteristics that are not currently commercially available, allowing a buyout of CO$_2$ emissions (as opposed to capping emissions to zero), and not considering other cost-effective firm generation sources.
elements.sent-2411-535: Disallowing CCS also raises the social buyout cost of CO$_2$ emissions, as represented by the NPV of the CO$_2$ buyout price over the whole study, by 200 percent (\$378 billion) relative to the base case.
elements.sent-2411-536: About 20\% of this extra cost is recovered in the form of lower expenditure for fuel and equipment.

elements.sent-2411-547: Although different models and configurations produced substantially similar-cost capacity expansion plans (generally within about 10\% across all configurations and models), the details of these plans and their emission levels can vary quite strongly.
elements.sent-2411-548: This reflects an overall principle observed in this work that there are a range of solutions that can have similar costs, so users should not assume that the details of any particular plan are set in stone.

elements.sent-2411-550: The treatment of retirement consistently impacts emissions across models but in ways that differ by scenario.
elements.sent-2411-551: In the current policy scenario, economic retirement leads to an immediate retirement of about 50\% of existing coal generation, which explains most of the estimated emission reductions estimated to occur before 2040.
elements.sent-2411-552: While the affected generators otherwise remain serviceable, their operating costs are relatively high because they are generally less efficient.
elements.sent-2411-553: Beyond 2040, cost reductions in batteries, conventional natural gas generation, and CCS outcompete nuclear, facilitating the economic retirement of nuclear capacity and increasing emissions.
elements.sent-2411-554: In the net-zero scenario, economic retirement leads to a near immediate elimination of coal generation and approximately 30\% reduction in natural gas generation, explaining much of the impact on emissions.
elements.sent-2411-555: The lost fossil generation is complemented by an increase in battery use and preservation of hydroelectric generation that otherwise would retire due to age.

elements.sent-2411-559: While the affected generators otherwise remain serviceable, the models find that it is more economically efficient to reduce total capacity and run the remaining plants at higher capacity factors.

In the hours where it is most cost-effective to meet demand with thermal capacity, newer and more efficient units can be used. Most existing coal immediately retires when allowed. Slightly less transmission built In 2027, endogenous retirement enables emissions reductions nearly equal to the base case. Raising coal prices by \$0.50/MMBTU leads to the retirement of all but 10 GW coal capacity, which operates at a lower capacity factor. The generation shortfall is made up with natural gas and solar, leading to sustained emissions reductions compared to reference fuel prices. Slightly more transmission built Short Sample Period Nationally, most models have less generation from wind and more from CCS when using fewer sample weeks. Emissions differ by less than 5\% in all model periods. Larger relative differences in capacity and generation decisions are seen in regional results. Nationally, all models have less wind and more solar generation when using fewer sample weeks. Emissions vary across models more than in the Net-zero scenario. They also vary more from 52-week configuration, and are generally higher. Larger relative differences in capacity and generation decisions are seen in regional results. Multi-Period Foresight Small changes from myopic, but not consistent across models. GenX has less CCS generation, more from Wind/Solar. USENSYS is the opposite. GenX builds more transmission (capacity, not sure about GW-miles) in foresight, Switch/USENSYS build less. TEMOA results suggest that foresight leads to less wind/solar through 2035, then a large build-out in 2040 while plants are still able to qualify for the PTC. GenX builds more wind and solar in early periods than TEMOA, but sees a similar build-out in 2040$\backslash$. The models disagree about if emissions will be higher than the myopic configuration before 2040$\backslash$. \br \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-566: The results from this intercomparison study provide significant insights for policymakers, researchers, and users of electricity CEMs interested in guiding the U.S.. power sector’s decarbonization.
elements.sent-2411-567: Our findings confirm that harmonizing model inputs and carefully differentiating between scenarios, configurations, and model structures can enhance the interpretability and robustness of model outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-568: These practices help stakeholders make more confident and informed decisions in energy policy and system planning.
elements.sent-2411-569: We summarize a few key findings from the more detailed descriptions in \Sref{section:results .}
elements.sent-2411-570: Models agree that a net-zero scenario is more expensive, has higher emissions, and relies more on inter-regional transmission when CCS is not available.
elements.sent-2411-571: Without CCS, models project wind, solar, and battery storage as a least-cost substitute as opposed to nuclear power.
elements.sent-2411-572: Generators were assumed to retire either economically (when operating costs exceed revenue) or when they age out at the end of their useful service life, and these assumptions were the most influential configuration feature.
elements.sent-2411-573: Economic retirement results in lower operational costs in both the current policies and net-zero scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-574: The retirement configuration also impacts investment decisions and emissions, with impacts that vary by scenario.
elements.sent-2411-575: Relative to aging out, assuming plants retire economically halves the existing coal fleet for current policies in the short run and nearly eliminates coal under the net-zero scenarios over the entire modeled duration.
elements.sent-2411-576: Assuming current policies in the long-run, however, economic retirement retains more coal capacity, in part to make up for nuclear plants that retire in 2040 when existing subsidies expire.
elements.sent-2411-577: Absent more aggressive decarbonization policies or additional economic pressures\footnote{see \ref{appendix:fuel_prices for a discussion of how fuel prices affect coal, nuclear, and natural gas capacity with economic retirement , the results imply that the economic stress on coal plants may be short-lived.}}
elements.sent-2411-578: The current policy scenario is sensitive to whether investment decisions are assumed to be made myopically or with foresight.
elements.sent-2411-579: With foresight, wind and solar expand less in the initial periods in anticipation of falling costs, then grow much faster in the final period where subsidies are available.
elements.sent-2411-580: The myopic configuration has a slower rate of expansion, and ultimately builds less wind and solar capacity.
elements.sent-2411-581: However, configuring foresight is more complex such that it can force modeling trade offs.
elements.sent-2411-582: Here, we model myopic and foresight configurations assuming 52-week and 20-week temporal resolutions, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-583: We find that the information gained with foresight does not necessarily compensate for the loss of temporal detail, with investment decisions from the 52-week myopic models often yielding lower system costs and less non-served energy than the 20-week foresight models.
elements.sent-2411-584: Preventing any expansion of transmission increases 2050 operational costs by 4.6\% and emissions by 55\%.
elements.sent-2411-585: This implies that it is possible to substitute for transmission when decarbonizing but at the expense of an ‘unmet policy penalty’.
elements.sent-2411-586: However, the total penalty appears to be modest.
elements.sent-2411-587: The assets that substitute for transmission vary regionally based upon resource adequacy, existing assets, and prices.
elements.sent-2411-588: The difference in total system costs between configurations is often lower smaller than differences in investment decisions and emissions.
elements.sent-2411-589: Harmonization has the important spillover benefit of distilling residual but important model differences, which deserve equal merit to the above consensus-based findings.
elements.sent-2411-590: After harmonization, models still retained differences in the algebraic formulations of their native optimization routines.
elements.sent-2411-591: Some important differences include alternative dispatch configurations (the ability to simulate unit commitment), configurations of downtime, the co-optimization of storage power and capacity, transmission expansion constraints, carbon pricing, treating service life differently than capital recovery, and whether technologies vary by vintage or operational parameters vary by period.
elements.sent-2411-592: These residual differences were not sufficiently influential to preclude harmonizing our base configurations, but we would expect more differences between models if they were not forced into such strict alignment via data inputs.
elements.sent-2411-593: We emphasize that coding technology vintages and allowing operations to vary by period were particularly useful when parameterizing the complex subsidy designs in the PTC and ITC.
elements.sent-2411-594: We reflect upon our harmonization effort to offer lessons learned for other groups engaged in similar future efforts.
elements.sent-2411-595: We found carefully differentiating between scenarios (representing different policy alternatives) from model configurations (alternative model resolutions and technical abstractions of the energy system) extremely helpful during project execution and for communicating results.
elements.sent-2411-596: The project also benefited significantly from a shared data pipeline to render consistent model inputs.
elements.sent-2411-597: The pipeline not only improved efficiency but developing it led to a shared understanding of each model’s assumptions and methods.
elements.sent-2411-598: There were considerable challenges in accommodated the various native model input formats with a data pipeline that was originally designed for one model (GenX).\footnote{Many of the difficulties with harmonizing data were related to translating operating parameters such as ramp rates, or labeling technologies as eligible for economic retirement.}
elements.sent-2411-599: Determining when differences in results were due to a missed or mis-interpreted model parameter rather than underlying model differences -- and finding the errant parameter -- was an especially difficult task.
elements.sent-2411-600: If we had known that model results would aligned so well across nearly all configurations, we would have focused harder on identifying input differences in data pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-601: Finally, the project benefited from an extremely democratic and consensus-oriented decision making process that allowed the open discovery of models’ strengths, weaknesses, and differences.
elements.sent-2411-602: This process yielded positive results, as all models improved -- some even incorporating previously unconfigured features -- and all modeling teams gained a deeper understanding of various approaches to representing real-world complexities within a mathematical framework.
elements.sent-2411-603: We see tremendous value in the consensus forming nature of model intercomparisons and harmonization.
elements.sent-2411-604: Energy and climate policy decisions can easily be made brittle when research findings differ, particularly when differences remain unexplained or not well understood by decision makers.
elements.sent-2411-605: Model intercomparisons offer one strategy for forming consensus.
elements.sent-2411-606: Other strategies, such as forming standards, disclaimers, or decisions by committees, may also be of value.
elements.sent-2411-607: As part of this effort, we developed a pipeline to render consistent input databases, a step towards forming a standard that appears to be aligned with parallel efforts by others.
elements.sent-2411-608: Importantly, any setting of standards should be consensus oriented in and of themselves to ensure participation.

elements.sent-2411-615: As part of the Inflation Reduction Act, new zero-carbon resources are eligible for a production tax credit for the first 10 years of operation.

elements.sent-2411-632: Harmonizing inputs across diverse power system models revealed several key insights into how model design influences input requirements and configuration flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-633: Each model’s input structure is shaped by its development context and intended applications, leading to differences in how they handle planning period setups, resource retirements, and cost specifications.
elements.sent-2411-634: For instance, GenX, which typically operates in a myopic mode, organizes its inputs as separate files for each planning period.
elements.sent-2411-635: This setup simplifies modifying operational parameters, such as variable O\&M, across periods but complicates tracking capacity vintages within single resources.
elements.sent-2411-636: As a result, GenX often relies on economic retirement instead of age-based retirements, and power plant ages aren’t tracked, limiting certain analyses.
elements.sent-2411-637: Switch, by contrast, is more commonly run in foresight mode and accommodates age-based retirements.
elements.sent-2411-638: Its input structure separates period-specific resource information from data constant across periods, facilitating long-term projections.
elements.sent-2411-639: While Switch can be adapted to a myopic setup by using multiple input files, this approach isn’t standard, which may affect ease of harmonization when comparing foresight and myopic models directly.
elements.sent-2411-640: Temoa provides more extensive support for resource vintages and operating parameters, particularly useful for age-based retirements and variable cost changes over time, such as those influenced by production tax credits.
elements.sent-2411-641: With inputs stored in a single SQLite database, Temoa indexes parameters by both model period and technology vintage, though this level of detail can result in model “bloat.” Temoa’s foresight and optional limited myopic capabilities allow it to handle both economic and age-based retirements, and its ability to specify parameters such as WACC and O\&M costs by vintage aids in precision.
elements.sent-2411-642: However, unlike GenX, Temoa does not co-optimize power and energy capacities for storage, requiring users to specify different storage durations as separate resources.
elements.sent-2411-643: USENSYS, built on the energyRt platform, provides substantial flexibility for model configuration, facilitating adaptation to project-specific assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-644: Its modular structure allows for a three-part representation of energy storage—"charger," "accumulator," and "discharger"—optimized independently for each storage capacity parameter.
elements.sent-2411-645: USENSYS applies an age-based framework for retirements, with options for early retirement, and can track capacities at various levels, treating vintages with unique parameters as distinct technologies.
elements.sent-2411-646: While designed for foresight applications, modifications in USENSYS support myopic optimization and time-sliced sub-annual modeling, expanding its flexibility but introducing complexity in harmonization with other models.
elements.sent-2411-647: PowerGenome served as the primary data pipeline across models, yet harmonizing these inputs highlighted challenges, particularly where certain input data were relevant to only one model’s configuration.
elements.sent-2411-648: For example, GenX uses ramp rate limits only when unit commitment constraints are active, so this data might remain inactive in other configurations.
elements.sent-2411-649: A clearer distinction of which data inputs were essential to each configuration would have improved efficiency, reducing early misunderstandings and clarifying assumptions in the intercomparison process.
elements.sent-2411-650: Ultimately, these differences underscore the importance of understanding model-specific input requirements when harmonizing for inter-model comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-651: For model users, documenting the relevance and function of each input field for each scenario and configuration can streamline cross-model integration and enhance interpretability across studies..

elements.sent-2411-653: This study performs a detailed intercomparison of four open-source electricity capacity expansion models—Temoa, Switch, GenX, and USENSYS—to evaluate 1) how closely the results of these models align when inputs and configurations are harmonized, and 2) the degree to which varying model configurations affect outputs.
elements.sent-2411-654: We harmonize the inputs to each model using PowerGenome and use clearly defined scenarios (policy conditions) and configurations (model setup choices).
elements.sent-2411-655: This allows us to isolate how differences in model structure affect policy outcomes and investment decisions.
elements.sent-2411-656: Our framework allows each model to be tested on identical assumptions for policy, technology costs, and operational constraints, allowing us to focus on differences that arise from inherent model structures.
elements.sent-2411-657: Key findings highlight that, when harmonized, models produce very similar capacity portfolios under current policies and net-zero scenarios, with less than 1\% difference in system costs for most configurations.
elements.sent-2411-658: This agreement among models allows us to focus on how configuration choices affect model results.
elements.sent-2411-659: For instance, configurations with unit commitment constraints or economic retirement yield different investments and system costs compared to simpler configurations.
elements.sent-2411-660: Our findings underscore the importance of aligning input data and transparently defining scenarios and configurations to provide robust policy insights.
elements.sent-2411-661: To meet this challenge, stakeholders need tools to help plan for deep decarbonization of power systems on a national scale \cite{deep_decarbonization_pathways_project_pathways_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-662: Better models can help planners and policymakers prioritize investments that support rapid and reliable decarbonization while minimizing costs and avoiding risks such as premature obsolescence.
elements.sent-2411-663: A new generation of well-established open-source electricity system capacity expansion models (CEMs) has been created to aid both in designing power systems served by high levels of renewable energy and in modeling a diverse range of energy pathways.
elements.sent-2411-664: They are often used to study the impact of national-level policy decisions.
elements.sent-2411-665: Although these models may use many of the same underlying methods and constraints, differences in input data, model setup, or configurations--choices of model features, resolution, etc.--can lead to divergent findings.
elements.sent-2411-666: Both input and structural assumptions can lead to different model results.
elements.sent-2411-667: Commodity prices and infrastructure costs are examples of input assumptions that may vary by model but can be relatively easy to harmonize.
elements.sent-2411-668: The method of determining asset retirement, such as aging out assets at the end of their service life versus retiring assets based on economic uncompetitiveness, is a structural assumption.
elements.sent-2411-669: Methods to treat within-model input uncertainty and variability, such as sensitivity and Monte Carlo analysis, are both mature and common.
elements.sent-2411-670: These techniques examine how model results vary with differing input assumptions and, in doing so, can identify which input parameters are most influential or what results are robust to varying model inputs.
elements.sent-2411-671: In contrast, studies rarely perform experiments under a range of different structural configurations, partly because of the challenge of identifying varying structural methods from the perspective of a singular model.
elements.sent-2411-672: Intermodel comparisons can broaden the scope of structural and input modeling choices to encompass those made across different modeling teams.
elements.sent-2411-673: Previous intermodel comparison efforts, such as those by the Stanford Energy Modeling Forum \cite{huntington1982modeling have been instrumental in developing and applying methods to fairly align policy scenarios assumptions across models.}
elements.sent-2411-674: However, these efforts have not yet fully harmonized respective input assumptions or structural differences, which limits the ability to isolate the precise drivers of divergent model outcomes \cite{henry_promoting_2021, van_ouwerkerk_impacts_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-675: Henry et al. \cite{henry_promoting_2021 highlighted the often untested influence of structural differences--such as treatment of technology characteristics and optimization approaches--but only for a single simulation period, relatively simplified scenarios, and for only five greenfield generation technologies.}
elements.sent-2411-676: Similarly, Sánchez-Pérez et al. \cite{sanchez-perez_effect_2022 depicts how the treatment of time drastically affects the optimal investment and operation of long-duration storage, and Jacobson et al. \cite{jacobsonQuantifyingImpactEnergy2024 demonstrates that renewable siting decisions can be suboptimal when using lower temporal resolution.}}
elements.sent-2411-677: Hoffmann et al. \cite{hoffmann_review_2024 emphasize that developing shared mathematical structures and consistent modeling frameworks can facilitate clearer communication and understanding between different models, promoting transparency and comparability.}
elements.sent-2411-678: These studies suggest that not only does it remain difficult to fairly compare model results, but that there is not yet consensus on how to design and execute intercomparison studies.
elements.sent-2411-679: To address this gap, our study performs a rigorous comparison of four open-source, decarbonization-focused electricity capacity expansion models: Tools for Energy Model Optimization and Analysis (TEMOA)\cite{hunter_modeling_2013 , Switch\cite{johnston_switch_2019 , GenX\cite{jenkins_genx_2024 , and United States Energy SYStem (USENSYS)\cite{lugovoy_usensys_2024 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-680: Unlike previous comparisons, we explicitly harmonize input data (using the PowerGenome data tool \cite{schivley_powergenomepowergenome_2024 ).}
elements.sent-2411-681: We systematically test different structural configurations across several relevant policy scenarios, and compare results across models and configurations within each of these scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-682: This approach enables us to clearly distinguish how structural modeling choices and assumptions influence model outcomes and investment decisions, and to identify residual differences attributable to the models’ internal formulations.
elements.sent-2411-683: It also allows us to identify which model features (as chosen in each configuration) are most important for studying different types of scenarios (e.g., current policies versus net-zero decarbonization).
elements.sent-2411-684: Our quantitative results enable us to draw highly specific and defensible conclusions about the sources of differences among modeling efforts.
elements.sent-2411-685: This approach allows us to provide clear and confident recommendations for policy decisions.
elements.sent-2411-686: In addition to presenting quantitative model results we also use the intercomparison process to suggest guidelines and more generalizable methods for performing modeling intercomparisons.
elements.sent-2411-687: The scenarios we model are of general interest and are not intended to be comprehensive.
elements.sent-2411-688: They are designed to test how different input assumptions and model configurations can impact the outcomes, and thus the policy recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-689: For instance, our net-zero scenario uses emission targets derived from a model that includes hydrogen production via electrolysis.
elements.sent-2411-690: However, we do not include the resulting electricity demand -- or any endogenous production of hydrogen -- in our systems.
elements.sent-2411-691: We do not explore strict zero-emission requirements without a buyout price, demand growth from data centers, the potential role of long-duration energy storage, alternative firm energy technologies, or the many other relevant policies, technologies, or interactions of the power system with the broader economy.
elements.sent-2411-692: These topics are not among the objectives of this effort.
elements.sent-2411-693: The main objective of this work is to perform an intercomparison of models with harmonized model inputs and explore the degree to which varying model configurations affect output.
elements.sent-2411-694: We harmonized and compared four energy system models representing the electric power system for the continental US: GenX, Switch, USENSYS, and TEMOA.
elements.sent-2411-695: All four models employ optimization to select the least cost set of energy sources and technologies for energy conversion, transmission, storage, and carbon management that meet electricity demand on an hourly basis, subject to exogenously specified costs, policies, and scenario assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-696: The study relies on the concepts of scenarios (the world we optimize for) and configurations (choices of time and space resolution, technology availability, and model features to use when setting up the model).
elements.sent-2411-697: The scenarios are used to represent policy questions of interest.
elements.sent-2411-698: In general, users get an answer to a policy question by comparing scenarios to each other, e.g., comparing emissions and costs between the deep-decarbonization scenario and the current policies scenario.
elements.sent-2411-699: Configurations allow us to distinguish between the effect of choices users make when setting up their models and differences in how features are implemented in the models themselves.
elements.sent-2411-700: In other comparison studies, much of what is perceived as “model differences” are actually due to different configuration choices made by different modeling teams.
elements.sent-2411-701: The models might produce much more similar results if they were run using the same configurations.
elements.sent-2411-702: We avoid this ambiguity by using the same configurations for all models.
elements.sent-2411-703: This allows us to identify which specific features are important for effectively modeling each scenario, and also to assess the extent to which internal differences between the models drive differences in results.
elements.sent-2411-704: In addition to the assumptions listed in \Tref{table2 , we clustered fossil-based generators within each region based on their heat rate and fixed operating costs.}
elements.sent-2411-705: Clustering reduces the dimensionality of models while preserving the underlying variability in resources.

Model periods are 2024-27, 2028-30, and then every 5 years through 2050. Investment decisions are made to meet demand in the final year of each model period. Unit Commitment and Ramping Base configuration using linearized unit commitment; including restrictions on ramp rate, min load, up and down time, and startup costs. Economic Retirement Base configuration without age-based retirement of any existing plants; instead we retire generators permanently if the operating costs are higher than the revenue; avoidable costs include fixed O\&M but not capital recovery; capital recovery is amortized over normal life, but then continues through the whole study at the same rate. Short Sample Period Base configuration using 20 weeks sampled from full weather period (with probability weights) instead of 52 weeks to reduce the model size. Multi-Period Foresight Short Sample Period configuration where we co-optimize all 6 model periods together (short sample is needed to reduce allow for multi-period). Operational Simulation Specialized case used to evaluate the performance and system cost of proposed plans in a common context. Base configuration, but freeze generation \& transmission decisions based on a previously run capacity expansion model, use limits from the Unit Commitment and Ramping configuration, and consider all 52 weeks of weather. Then run the model to simulate performance of the plan through the year. Any generation shortfalls are priced at \$5,000/MWh. \br \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-715: Calculating model costs: One purpose of this study is to show which configurations are most effective in identifying low-cost solutions in each scenario.
elements.sent-2411-716: Differences in how each model calculates and reports costs make it difficult to directly compare using outputs from each model.
elements.sent-2411-717: To address this difficulty, we run an operational simulation using a single model (GenX) with fixed capacity decisions, a full year of weather data, and constraints on cycling and ramp rates to calculate the cost of adopting the portfolio proposed by each model.
elements.sent-2411-718: We first demonstrate the effectiveness of our harmonization approach by comparing outcomes from the models under fully harmonized inputs and base-case assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-719: The net present values of the operational model system costs for the current policy and net-zero scenarios vary by 0.2-0.3\% across the models, demonstrating that models are all finding nearly equivalent global cost minimums.
