Subplots show total capacity (top-left) and generation (top-right) of selected resources, total transmission capacity (bottom-left), and annual operational system cost (bottom-right).} \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-720: Next, we examine how model results differ across various policy scenarios, illustrating the impacts of varying scenario assumptions such as carbon buyout prices, transmission constraints, and technology availability.
elements.sent-2411-721: Our results suggest that current policies are insufficient to achieve deep and sustained emission reductions, similar to previous studies \cite{bistline_emissions_2023, bistline_power_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-722: Among the tested net-zero child scenarios, the carbon buyout price has the most substantial impact on system emissions.
elements.sent-2411-723: A buyout price of \$200 per ton results in slowly decreasing emissions after 2030.
elements.sent-2411-724: Even by 2050, none of the models find emissions dropping below 100 million tonnes per year.
elements.figure-2411-033: \includegraphics[scale=1,trim={0cm 0cm 0 0cm},clip]{figures/paper_emissions_smaller_font.pdf} \caption{Projected annual emissions within each planning period for the current policies and net-zero emissions scenarios. The color of the lines indicates current policies versus net-zero cases; the shape of the marker indicates CO$_2$ buyout price; dashed lines indicate limits on transmission expansion; and marker color indicates whether CCS is allowed. The base net-zero configuration is indicated by a thicker blue line. } \label{fig2}
elements.sent-2411-725: The ability to build inter-regional transmission represents a policy choice; we find that constraining transmission expansion increases emissions and costs, as shown in \Fref{fig3 .}
elements.sent-2411-726: Specifically, preventing all transmission expansion increases 2050 operational costs by roughly 4.6\% and increases emissions (approximately 55\%) due to reliance on fossil-based generation.
elements.sent-2411-727: Relative to unconstrained expansion, preventing expansion increases operational costs in 2050 by 4.6\% and increases emissions by 55\%.
elements.sent-2411-728: The most substantial substitute for transmission is battery capacity, but generation mixes and quantities also change within regions.
elements.figure-2411-034: \includegraphics[scale=1,trim={0cm 0cm 0 0cm},clip]{figures/paper_transmission_emissions_cost_small_font.pdf} \caption{Tighter constraints on transmission expansion increase both system costs and emissions. Results are identical in all constraint child scenarios prior to 2030. Costs and emissions are annual values for each planning period.} \label{fig3}
elements.sent-2411-729: Without transmission constraints, the models prefer to build additional CCS and solar capacity in PJMW.
elements.sent-2411-730: When they cannot expand transmission between PJMW and PJME, they build more solar capacity in PJME.
elements.sent-2411-731: Unconstrained transmission favors the expansion of wind capacity in SPPS.
elements.sent-2411-732: When transmission out of SPPS is constrained, the model substitutes modest amounts of wind, solar, and CCS in adjacent regions.
elements.figure-2411-035: \includegraphics[scale=1,trim={0cm 0cm 0 0cm},clip]{figures/paper_transmission_cap.pdf} \caption{Trade-offs between expansions of transmission and capacity of CCS, solar, and wind resources in select modeled regions as estimated by GenX for 2050. Results reflect differences in resource capacity and transmission capacity between unconstrained and no (0\%) transmission expansion. Transmission constraints increase resource capacity in the purple regions and decrease it in the green regions.} \label{fig4}
elements.sent-2411-733: Finally, we consider the scenario where carbon capture and storage (CCS) is unavailable, testing its role as a key firm generation source under deep decarbonization constraints.
elements.sent-2411-734: This results in about 134 million tons more emissions in 2050, 122\% higher than when CCS is available.
elements.sent-2411-735: Disallowing CCS also raises the NPV social buyout cost of CO$_2$ emissions by around 200 percent (\$378 billion) relative to the base case.
elements.sent-2411-736: About 20\% of this extra CO$_2$ cost is offset in the form of lower total expenditures on fuel and equipment.
elements.figure-2411-036: \includegraphics[scale=1,trim={0cm 0cm 0 0cm},clip]{figures/paper_ccs_results_small_font.pdf} \caption{Effects of allowing or disallowing CCS on total capacity, generation mix, emissions, transmission expansion, and costs under the Net-zero scenario. Capacity and transmission represent existing stock at the end of each planning period. Generation and costs are annual values within each planning period.} \label{fig5}
elements.sent-2411-737: Having explored the differences across scenarios, we now examine how configuration choices—such as retirement policies, temporal sampling, and foresight—affect model outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-738: Using economic retirement instead of age-based retirement is the most important single feature that we tested that allows models to find a solution with lower operational costs in both the current policies and deep decarbonization scenarios.
elements.figure-2411-037: \includegraphics[scale=1,trim={0cm 0cm 0 0cm},clip]{figures/paper_configuration_costs_small_font.pdf} \caption{Annual expenditure per planning period in 52-week operational simulation for each model configuration, assuming net-zero emissions (top panel) and current policies (bottom panel). The base configuration uses a solid brown line with square, black markers, indicating age-based retirement, simplified dispatch, 52 sample weeks and myopic outlook. Alternative configurations test deviations from each of these.} \label{fig6}
elements.sent-2411-739: Although different models and configurations produced substantially similar cost capacity expansion plans within each scenario (generally within about 10\% across all configurations and models), the details of these plans and their emission levels can vary quite strongly.
elements.sent-2411-740: This reflects an overall principle observed in this work that a range of solutions can all have similar costs, so users should not assume that the details of any particular plan are set in stone.
elements.sent-2411-741: In the current policy scenario, economic retirement initially reduces emissions by retiring more than half of existing coal capacity.
elements.sent-2411-742: Beginning in 2040, after the phase out of existing policies, the economic retirement of nuclear plants, coupled with retained coal capacity, results in higher emissions relative to the base configuration.
elements.sent-2411-743: In the net-zero scenario, economic retirement leads to a near immediate elimination of coal generation and approximately 30\% reduction in natural gas generation, which explains much of the impact on emissions.
elements.sent-2411-744: The lost fossil generation is complemented by an increase in battery use and preservation of hydroelectric capacity that otherwise would retire due to age.

The base configuration uses a solid brown line with square, black markers, indicating age-based retirement, simplified dispatch, 52 sample weeks and myopic outlook. Alternative configurations test deviations from each of these.} \label{fig7}
elements.sent-2411-745: Similarly, 40\% of existing nuclear capacity is economically retired in the planning period 2040 after their subsidies expire.

elements.sent-2411-746: The results of this intercomparison study provide significant insights for policymakers, researchers, and users of electricity CEMs interested in guiding the US power sector’s decarbonization.
elements.sent-2411-747: Our findings confirm that harmonizing model inputs and carefully differentiating between scenarios, configurations, and model structures can improve the interpretability and robustness of model outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-748: Given the input cost assumptions, the models agree that a net-zero scenario is more expensive, has higher emissions, and relies more on inter-regional transmission when CCS is not available.
elements.sent-2411-749: Without CCS, the models select wind, solar, and battery storage as a least-cost substitute as opposed to nuclear power.
elements.sent-2411-750: Economic retirement reduces operational costs in both the current policies and net-zero scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-751: It also impacts investment decisions and emissions, especially in the current policies scenario.
elements.sent-2411-752: Allowing plants to retire economically immediately halves the existing coal fleet for current policies in the short run and nearly eliminates coal under the net-zero scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-753: Assuming current policies remain unchanged through 2050, however, economic retirement retains more coal capacity, in part to make up for nuclear plants that retire in 2040 when existing subsidies expire.
elements.sent-2411-754: However, foresight models are larger and require more computational resources because they account for investment and operational decisions across multiple periods, which can force modeling trade offs.
elements.sent-2411-755: Preventing any expansion of inter-regional transmission (net-zero scenario only) increases 2050 operational costs by 4.6\% and emissions by 55\%.
elements.sent-2411-756: This implies that it is possible to substitute for transmission when decarbonizing, but at the expense of an ‘unmet policy penalty’.
elements.sent-2411-757: The difference in total system costs between configurations is often less than the differences in investment decisions and emissions.
elements.sent-2411-758: After harmonization, the models still retained differences in the algebraic formulations of their native optimization routines.
elements.sent-2411-759: Some important differences include alternative dispatch configurations (the ability to simulate unit commitment), configurations of downtime, the co-optimization of storage power and capacity, and whether technologies vary by vintage or operational parameters vary by period.
elements.sent-2411-760: We emphasize that coding technology vintages and allowing operations to vary by period were particularly useful when parameterizing the complex subsidy designs of current policy production tax credits.
elements.sent-2411-761: The project also benefited from a shared data pipeline to ensure consistent inputs across models.
elements.sent-2411-762: The pipeline improved efficiency and led to a better understanding of the assumptions and methods of each model.
elements.sent-2411-763: There were considerable challenges in accommodating the various native model input formats with a data pipeline that was originally designed for one model (GenX).\footnote{Many of the difficulties with harmonizing data were related to translating operating parameters such as ramp rates, or labeling technologies as eligible for economic retirement.}
elements.sent-2411-764: Determining when differences in results were due to a missed or misinterpreted model parameter rather than underlying model differences---and finding the errant parameter---was an especially difficult task.
elements.sent-2411-765: If we had known that model results would align so well across nearly all configurations, we would have focused more on identifying input differences in data pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-766: Finally, the project benefited from a democratic and consensus-oriented decision-making process that enabled open discovery of the strengths, weaknesses, and differences of the models.
elements.sent-2411-767: This process yielded positive results, as all models improved---some even incorporating previously unconfigured features---and all modeling teams gained a deeper understanding of various approaches to representing real-world complexities within a mathematical framework.
elements.sent-2411-768: We see tremendous value in the consensus-forming nature of model intercomparisons and harmonization.
elements.sent-2411-769: When research findings about specific energy or climate policies differ across studies, policy decisions regarding whether or how to implement these policies may lack robustness—particularly if differences remain unexplained or if policymakers poorly understand how strongly results depend on model configuration choices.
elements.sent-2411-770: Model intercomparisons, in which models use common input data and transparently document their configuration choices, offer one strategy to improve understanding and build consensus around robust results.
elements.sent-2411-771: Other strategies, such as establishing standards, including clear disclaimers, or forming decisions through committees, may also be valuable.
elements.sent-2411-772: As part of this effort, we developed a pipeline to render consistent input databases—a step toward establishing a common standard that aligns well with parallel efforts by others.
elements.sent-2411-773: Importantly, any standard-setting process should itself be consensus-oriented to ensure broad participation.. .
elements.sent-2411-774: Switch and GenX, which are not able to vintage new build resource, convert the PTC to an ITC.
elements.sent-2411-775: The net-zero CO$_2$ targets used in this study are based on results from the REPEAT study's Net-Zero Pathway \cite{jenkins_climate_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-776: REPEAT models emissions across the entire US energy system.
elements.sent-2411-777: The Net-Zero Pathway finds that the most cost-effective place to reduce emissions in the near term is in the power system.
elements.sent-2411-778: While REPEAT allows some emissions from the power sector in 2050, we modified the trajectory using a linear target to zero emissions from 2030 to 2050.
elements.sent-2411-779: Our 2027 target of 847 million tonnes CO$_2$ is erroneously based on the 2025 REPEAT value -- the results in 2027 are 494 million tonnes CO$_2$.
